key_insights.insight: Cumulative experiences, not just single traumatic events, shape a person most profoundly.
key_insights.quote: In the same way, I think the worst traumas that I actually endured weren't any single moments. They were cumulative. They were things that happened over time.

key_insights.insight: Holding onto anger leads to resentment and bitterness, which can negatively determine the course of one's life.
key_insights.quote: I think holding onto anger, you know, it becomes resentment and that leads to bitterness and, and bitter is not something I'm interested in being.

key_insights.insight: It's important to be resilient, and therapy during non-crisis times can help build that resilience.
key_insights.quote: The only time that you can learn or become resilient without going through trauma and surviving it is therapy when you're not at a point of crisis.

key_insights.insight: Worrying about things outside of your control distracts you from focusing on what's important and can negatively impact your mental health.
key_insights.quote: Worrying about what's out of your control and preempting things that may or may not happen is not the healthiest way to live. It's not great for your mental health. It's not great for the quality of life that you have.

key_insights.insight: Sometimes the most selfish thing you can do is focus on your own well being.
key_insights.quote: you have to fill up your own bottle so you can pour it out for other people

key_insights.insight: It's dangerous to pin your happiness on future achievements, as goalposts can always shift.
key_insights.quote: This is what's dangerous about pinning um your happiness or your your hopes of happiness on an ever-moving goalpost that quite often you move yourself

key_insights.insight: Perspective is key; stress can be stress, or it can be work, and anxiety can be anxiety or nervous energy.
key_insights.quote: Work can be stressed or work can just be work right it depends on your perception of it and so my anxiety can be anxiety or it can be nervous energy

key_insights.insight: Balance is key. Don't be afraid to indulge in some things you enjoy once in a while.
key_insights.quote: Me taking care of myself involves me having a blowout once in a while on my friends and and suffering the hangover that comes with it to remind myself why I don't do it all the time

actionable_advice.advice: Practice empathy and avoid quickly trying to fix someone's sadness.
actionable_advice.how_to: Allow them to be upset and go through their feelings rather than suppressing them.

actionable_advice.advice: Be proactive in caring for your physical and mental health
actionable_advice.how_to: Take a more proactive approach to my own health and that's not just my physical well-being that's my mental well-being ultimately they should be one in the same it's just health

actionable_advice.advice: Close the door on a relationship you need closure on.
actionable_advice.how_to: If you need closure close the door find closure in closing the door

actionable_advice.advice: Practice present moment awareness to enjoy experiences as they happen.
actionable_advice.how_to: Become entirely present, and don't worry about future obligations; have an empty head and an open heart.

memorable_stories: Professor Green describes his last conversation with his father before his father's death.
Professor Green describes the experience of being stabbed in the neck and calling his nan.
Stephen Bartlett shares a story of being hit in the head with a bottle at a club in New York.
contrarian_claims: The need for "closure" after a relationship is actually an insecurity about what happened
Often wanting to change the world is for the admiration of being seen as changing the world, not a genuine effort
episode_title: The Diary Of A CEO with Steven Bartlett - Professor Green - How To Overcome Life’s Hardest Challenges & Find A Purpose
